stem:
A 28-year-old woman in her second pregnancy presents to the birth suite at 39 weeks and 2 days with regular painful contractions every 4 minutes and ruptured membranes 30 minutes ago. Her booking visit was uncomplicated and her 36-week vaginal-rectal swab grew Streptococcus agalactiae. Her first child was born at term with no neonatal infection.

On examination her cervix is 5 cm dilated and the cephalic presenting part is engaged. Foetal heart rate is 138 with normal variability. She has no penicillin allergy.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.9 °C, foetal heart rate 138
data_table.Examination: Cervix 5 cm dilated, fully effaced, vertex at -1 station, clear liquor draining
data_table.Investigations: Antenatal 36-week vaginal-rectal GBS swab positive; FBC and group-and-hold within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Give a single dose of oral amoxicillin 1 g now and arrange home discharge until contractions intensify
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics do not achieve foetal-protective intrapartum levels reliably; intravenous benzylpenicillin is the standard. Home discharge with ruptured membranes in active labour is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

options.text: Withhold antibiotics and observe for clinical chorioamnionitis before treating as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis is offered based on the antenatal screen, not on clinical infection criteria; waiting for chorioamnionitis means accepting preventable early-onset neonatal GBS disease. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

options.text: Give intravenous clindamycin 900 mg 8-hourly until birth with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Clindamycin is an alternative agent for women with severe penicillin allergy and confirmed clindamycin susceptibility; in a non-allergic woman benzylpenicillin is first-line and provides better in-utero penetration. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

options.text: Commence intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose now, then 1.8 g every 4 hours until birth
options.rationale: RANZCOG and Therapeutic Guidelines recommend intrapartum benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery for any woman with a positive antenatal GBS screen presenting in labour or with rupture of membranes. The first dose is the most important; effective prophylaxis ideally requires at least 4 hours before delivery but should not be delayed. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

options.text: Defer antibiotic prophylaxis as her previous child was unaffected and prior unaffected pregnancy reduces neonatal risk
options.rationale: Prior unaffected pregnancy does not reduce GBS colonisation risk in the current pregnancy and does not waive the indication for prophylaxis with a current positive screen. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

explanation.summary: AU intrapartum GBS prophylaxis uses benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery for women with positive antenatal screen, prior infant with GBS disease, GBS bacteriuria in this pregnancy, or unknown status with risk factors. Clindamycin is reserved for confirmed severe penicillin allergy with susceptibility. Oral antibiotics are insufficient. The first dose is the most important; do not delay.
key_points: RANZCOG: IV benzylpenicillin 3 g load then 1.8 g 4-hourly until birth
Indications: positive screen, prior GBS infant, GBS bacteriuria, unknown + risk
Clindamycin only if severe penicillin allergy + susceptibility
First dose timing matters most; ideal ≥4 hours before delivery
explanation.pearls: Cefazolin replaces benzylpenicillin only for non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy; severe (anaphylaxis) allergy with confirmed clindamycin susceptibility uses clindamycin instead.

stem:
A 29-year-old woman attends your fertility clinic with 18 months of primary infertility. Her cycles are irregular, occurring every 45 to 90 days with light flow. She has hirsutism on the chin and lower abdomen, acne, and her BMI is 27 kg/m^2. Pelvic ultrasound shows enlarged ovaries with multiple peripheral follicles bilaterally. Her partner's semen analysis is normal.

Day-3 hormonal profile shows raised LH:FSH ratio, mildly raised testosterone, and normal prolactin and TSH. Hysterosalpingogram demonstrates patent fallopian tubes. She has been counselled on weight optimisation and lifestyle changes for 6 months without conception.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 124/78 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: BMI 27 kg/m^2, Ferriman-Gallwey 12, mild acne, no acanthosis nigricans
data_table.Investigations: FSH 5 IU/L (3-10), LH 14 IU/L (2-12), testosterone 2.6 nmol/L (<2.0), prolactin 380 mIU/L (60-560), TSH 1.8 mIU/L (0.4-4.0), AMH 64 pmol/L (12-49); HSG patent bilateral tubes; partner sperm density 32 million/mL with normal motility and morphology

lead_in: What would you first recommend as ovulation induction therapy?

options.text: Recombinant FSH gonadotrophin injections from cycle day 3 with transvaginal ultrasound monitoring
options.rationale: Gonadotrophins are second-line ovulation induction after oral agents fail and require close cycle monitoring because of multiple-pregnancy and ovarian hyperstimulation risks; they are not first-line in PCOS. (Source: RANZCOG PCOS)

options.text: Clomifene citrate 50 mg orally daily on cycle days 2-6 for up to six ovulatory cycles
options.rationale: Clomifene was historically first-line but letrozole has been shown superior in live-birth rates with similar safety in PCOS infertility and is now preferred. Clomifene remains a reasonable option where letrozole is not accessible. (Source: International PCOS Guideline)

options.text: Metformin extended-release 1500 mg daily as ovulation induction monotherapy
options.rationale: Metformin improves ovulation rates modestly and is used as an adjunct (particularly with BMI over 30) or where letrozole is contraindicated, but is not first-line as monotherapy for ovulation induction in this BMI band. (Source: International PCOS Guideline)

options.text: Letrozole 2.5 mg orally daily on cycle days 2-6 for up to six ovulatory cycles
options.rationale: The international PCOS guideline and RANZCOG endorse letrozole as first-line ovulation induction in PCOS-related anovulatory infertility, with higher live-birth rates than clomifene (PPCOS-II trial) and a lower multiple-pregnancy rate. The starting dose is 2.5 mg cycle days 2-6, titrated up to 7.5 mg if anovulatory, continued for up to six ovulatory cycles. (Source: International PCOS Guideline)

options.text: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling as the initial ovulation induction approach as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling is reserved for clomifene or letrozole resistance or where ovarian surgery is otherwise indicated; it is not a first-line approach because of surgical risk and uncertain durability. (Source: RANZCOG PCOS)

explanation.summary: Letrozole is the first-line ovulation induction agent for PCOS-related infertility per the 2023 International PCOS Guideline and RANZCOG. It outperforms clomifene in live-birth rate (PPCOS-II) with lower multiple-pregnancy risk. Metformin is an adjunct in higher-BMI women or alternative when letrozole is contraindicated. Gonadotrophins and ovarian drilling are second-line.
key_points: First-line: letrozole 2.5 mg cycle days 2-6, up to 6 cycles
Titrate to 7.5 mg if anovulatory
Clomifene second choice; metformin adjunct in BMI >30
Gonadotrophins and drilling for resistance
explanation.pearls: Letrozole has a shorter half-life than clomifene and avoids anti-oestrogenic endometrial effects, explaining the higher live-birth rate at equivalent ovulation rates.

stem:
A 30-year-old woman in her first pregnancy attends for her 11-week-plus-4-day dating ultrasound. The scan confirms a live twin pregnancy with two foetuses each measuring crown-rump length consistent with her last menstrual period. The intervening membrane meets the placental surface in a T-shape (no lambda sign), with a single placental mass and a thin intervening membrane. Both foetuses appear morphologically appropriate for gestation with present nasal bones and normal nuchal translucency measurements.

She is otherwise healthy with a BMI of 23 kg/m^2 and no significant medical or family history. She asks how her pregnancy will be monitored.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.5 °C
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, no visible scars
data_table.Investigations: Dating ultrasound 11+4 weeks: live twin pregnancy, T-sign at membrane-placenta interface, single placental mass, normal NT measurements bilaterally

lead_in: What is the most appropriate surveillance plan?

options.text: Four-weekly ultrasound from 20 weeks with growth assessment and delivery at 37-38 weeks as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Four-weekly surveillance starting at 20 weeks is the schedule for dichorionic-diamniotic twins; MCDA twins require fortnightly scans from 16 weeks because of the higher TTTS, sFGR, and TAPS risks. (Source: RANZCOG Twin Pregnancy)

options.text: Fortnightly ultrasound from 16 weeks with growth and amniotic fluid assessment, foetal Doppler studies and timed delivery at 36-37 weeks
options.rationale: T-sign at the membrane-placenta interface indicates a monochorionic-diamniotic (MCDA) twin pregnancy. RANZCOG and the AU monochorionic twin pathway recommend fortnightly ultrasound from 16 weeks to screen for twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (DVP discordance, bladder visualisation), selective foetal growth restriction (EFW discordance, UA Doppler), and TAPS (MCA-PSV); planned delivery is 36-37 weeks because of late stillbirth risk. (Source: RANZCOG Twin Pregnancy)

options.text: Routine antenatal care as for a singleton pregnancy with delivery at 40 weeks with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Twin pregnancies of any chorionicity require enhanced surveillance and earlier planned delivery because of increased perinatal mortality and morbidity compared to singletons. (Source: RANZCOG Twin Pregnancy)

options.text: Weekly cardiotocograph from 28 weeks with delivery at 34 weeks as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Routine CTG is not the surveillance modality for MCDA twins; ultrasound for foetal Doppler, growth and amniotic fluid is the primary surveillance. Delivery at 34 weeks is too early without specific complications. (Source: RANZCOG Twin Pregnancy)

options.text: Refer for early elective caesarean section at 32 weeks because of the chorionicity as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Uncomplicated MCDA twins do not require delivery at 32 weeks; this would impose iatrogenic prematurity. Planned delivery is at 36-37 weeks, mode determined by presentation and other obstetric factors. (Source: RANZCOG Twin Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: First-trimester ultrasound determines chorionicity reliably. T-sign = MCDA; lambda sign = DCDA. MCDA twins require fortnightly ultrasound from 16 weeks for TTTS, sFGR, and TAPS screening and planned delivery at 36-37 weeks. DCDA twins have four-weekly scans from 20 weeks and delivery at 37-38 weeks. Monochorionic-monoamniotic twins are managed with inpatient surveillance from 28-32 weeks and delivered at 32-34 weeks by caesarean.
key_points: T-sign at 11-14 weeks = monochorionic-diamniotic
MCDA: fortnightly US from 16 weeks; delivery 36-37 weeks
DCDA: four-weekly US from 20 weeks; delivery 37-38 weeks
MCMA: inpatient surveillance 28-32 weeks; caesarean 32-34 weeks
explanation.pearls: Lambda sign reflects placental tissue insinuating between two separate placentae (DCDA); T-sign reflects a single placental mass with a thin two-layer dividing membrane (MCDA).

stem:
A 32-year-old woman with a 4-year history of endometriosis is referred to your clinic with worsening cyclical pelvic pain, dyschezia, and rectal bleeding with menstruation over the past 6 months. She has tried a combined oral contraceptive in continuous regimen and the levonorgestrel IUS without adequate symptom control. Her CA125 is 65 U/mL.

Pelvic ultrasound shows a 4 cm right ovarian endometrioma and a 2 cm bowel wall hypoechoic mass at the rectosigmoid junction with loss of the normal hyperechoic submucosal layer. MRI confirms a 25 mm full-thickness rectal nodule infiltrating the muscularis and approaching the mucosa, with a separate uterosacral ligament nodule.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 80, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.5 °C
data_table.Examination: Tenderness in the pouch of Douglas, fixed retroverted uterus, no palpable rectal mass
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic ultrasound: 4 cm right ovarian endometrioma; 2 cm rectosigmoid bowel wall lesion. MRI: 25 mm deep infiltrating endometriotic nodule of anterior rectum + uterosacral nodule. CA125 65 U/mL (<35)
data_table.Medications: Continuous COC trial failed; levonorgestrel IUS trial failed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence GnRH agonist therapy with add-back tibolone as definitive management as the proposed approach
options.rationale: GnRH agonists can reduce pain in endometriosis but do not address deep infiltrating bowel disease anatomically; failure of two adequate hormonal trials and structural disease point to surgical planning. GnRH agonists may be used as a bridge to surgery, not as definitive management of DIE with bowel involvement. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis)

options.text: Refer to a tertiary endometriosis multidisciplinary clinic with colorectal and minimally invasive gynaecology input
options.rationale: Deep infiltrating endometriosis with bowel involvement requires multidisciplinary surgical planning; shaving versus disc excision versus segmental resection is decided by lesion size, depth, and proximity to mucosa with input from colorectal surgery, complex gynaecology, and radiology. RANZCOG and the AU endometriosis action plan direct DIE with bowel involvement to a specialised endometriosis MDT centre. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis)

options.text: Proceed with laparoscopic ablation of visible peritoneal endometriosis at the local hospital
options.rationale: Ablation alone is inadequate for deep infiltrating endometriosis; excision is required and bowel involvement warrants specialist colorectal-gynaecology MDT input rather than ablation at a non-specialist centre. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis)

options.text: Initiate aromatase inhibitor letrozole plus norethisterone as third-line hormonal therapy
options.rationale: Aromatase inhibitors have a role in refractory endometriosis but do not substitute for surgical evaluation of structural bowel disease, and require co-prescription with progestogen to prevent ovarian cyst formation in pre-menopausal women. They are not the next step here. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis)

options.text: Schedule total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy as first-line definitive surgery
options.rationale: At 32 years with fertility implications, hysterectomy is not first-line; conservative excisional surgery preserving fertility with MDT planning is preferred. Hysterectomy may be considered for women who have completed family with persistent symptoms after conservative surgery. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis)

explanation.summary: Deep infiltrating endometriosis with bowel involvement requires multidisciplinary care because surgical decisions (shave, disc, or segmental resection) depend on lesion size, depth, and proximity to mucosa, with colorectal and complex gynaecology input. Failed hormonal therapy with structural bowel disease is not adequately managed by additional medical therapy alone. Hysterectomy is rarely first-line in a 32-year-old.
key_points: DIE with bowel involvement = tertiary MDT clinic
Surgical options: shave / disc excision / segmental resection
Failed hormonal therapy + structural disease drives surgical referral
Hysterectomy is rarely first-line for fertility-aware patients
explanation.pearls: Cyclical rectal bleeding plus dyschezia in a woman with known endometriosis = highly suspicious for bowel DIE; MRI defines depth and shave-versus-resection planning.

stem:
A 34-year-old woman in her fourth pregnancy at 32 weeks gestation is referred to the maternal-foetal medicine unit. She has had three previous caesarean sections, all uncomplicated. Her current pregnancy was complicated by an anterior placenta praevia identified at the morphology scan. Detailed expert ultrasound at 28 weeks identified multiple placental lacunae, loss of the uteroplacental clear zone, retroplacental hypervascularity, and bridging vessels into the bladder. MRI confirms anterior placental tissue invading the bladder wall.

She is otherwise well, BMI 26 kg/m^2, has had a course of antenatal corticosteroids at 31 weeks, and her haemoglobin today is 124 g/L. She has been counselled on placenta percreta in the previous antenatal multidisciplinary meeting. She is asking when delivery should be planned, given she has small children at home.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88, BP 122/78 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C, foetal heart rate 142
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, three lower-segment caesarean scars, foetal cephalic presentation
data_table.Investigations: MRI: anterior placental tissue traversing myometrium with bladder wall invasion. Ultrasound: lacunae, loss of clear zone, hypervascularity, bridging vessels. Haemoglobin 124 g/L (115-160), platelets 232 x10^9/L (150-400), fibrinogen 4.2 g/L, group A positive, antibodies negative
data_table.Medications: Iron supplement, antenatal corticosteroids course completed at 31 weeks

lead_in: What would you first recommend regarding timing and mode of delivery?

options.text: Planned caesarean hysterectomy at 34-35 weeks gestation in a tertiary placenta accreta spectrum centre with interventional radiology standby, cross-matched blood, and pre-arranged urology input
options.rationale: RANZCOG and AU placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) pathway recommend planned delivery at 34-35 weeks for placenta percreta to balance preterm risk against the high risk of antepartum bleeding precipitating emergency surgery. Steroids have already been given at 31 weeks. Delivery must be in a specialised PAS centre with multidisciplinary team (anaesthesia, IR, urology, obstetrics, neonatology), cross-matched blood and cell salvage. Caesarean hysterectomy with placenta left in situ at delivery is the recommended primary approach for percreta. (Source: RANZCOG PAS)

options.text: Planned vaginal delivery at 39 weeks with active management of the third stage as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action as the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Placenta praevia alone contraindicates vaginal delivery; placenta percreta makes vaginal delivery actively dangerous because of catastrophic haemorrhage on placental separation. (Source: RANZCOG PAS)

options.text: Planned caesarean section at 39 weeks with manual removal of the placenta at delivery in the local maternity unit with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Manual placental removal in percreta causes massive haemorrhage; the recommended approach leaves the placenta in situ at hysterectomy. Local maternity units lack the multidisciplinary resources for PAS and term delivery without antepartum bleeding is unlikely. (Source: RANZCOG PAS)

options.text: Emergency caesarean section today regardless of foetal lung maturity to avoid antenatal haemorrhage as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: There is no current bleeding or other emergency indication; planned delivery at 34-35 weeks balances foetal maturity against bleeding risk. Emergency delivery at 32 weeks adds significant neonatal morbidity without immediate maternal benefit. (Source: RANZCOG PAS)

options.text: Conservative management with methotrexate and leaving the placenta in situ after caesarean delivery, with serial bhCG follow-up as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Conservative management leaving placenta in situ with delayed delivery and serial bhCG is an option in selected cases with future fertility desire and ability to monitor closely, but is high-risk in percreta with bladder invasion and is not first-line; methotrexate has no proven benefit. (Source: RANZCOG PAS)

explanation.summary: Placenta percreta with bladder invasion requires planned caesarean hysterectomy at 34-35 weeks in a specialised PAS centre with multidisciplinary preparation. The placenta is left in situ at the time of hysterectomy to avoid catastrophic haemorrhage. Antenatal corticosteroids precede delivery. Conservative methotrexate and term delivery approaches are not appropriate for bladder-invasive percreta. The disconfirming finding here is bladder wall invasion which kills conservative options and forces hysterectomy planning.
key_points: Percreta with bladder invasion = caesarean hysterectomy at 34-35 weeks
Placenta left in situ at hysterectomy
MDT centre: anaesthesia, IR, urology, obstetrics, neonatology
Antenatal corticosteroids; cross-match and cell salvage ready
explanation.pearls: Bladder invasion on MRI is the disconfirming finding that converts a 'maybe conservative' percreta into a mandatory caesarean hysterectomy at 34-35 weeks in a specialist centre.

stem:
A 44-year-old man with a 25-pack-year smoking history attends your GP clinic asking for help to quit. He has tried nicotine patches twice over the last 5 years with relapse within 4 weeks each time. He has a history of major depressive disorder, currently in remission for 18 months on sertraline 100 mg daily. He has no history of suicidal ideation or psychosis.

He is otherwise well, BMI 28 kg/m^2, with no cardiovascular disease, normal renal function, and no recent significant life events. He has set a quit date 2 weeks away and is keen to use pharmacotherapy with the highest evidence for success.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 132/82 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.5 °C
data_table.Examination: Mental state: euthymic mood, no suicidal ideation, no psychotic features, sleep and appetite unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: FBC and UEC within normal limits 3 months ago; eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73 m^2

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Bupropion sustained-release 150 mg daily for 3 days then 150 mg twice daily for 12 weeks
options.rationale: Bupropion is effective for smoking cessation but is lower in efficacy than varenicline and lowers seizure threshold; in a man already on an SSRI, varenicline is preferred because of better cessation rates and a more favourable interaction profile. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg daily for 3 days, then 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days, then 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks
options.rationale: RACGP smoking cessation guidance recommends varenicline as the most effective single agent; the EAGLES trial demonstrated no excess neuropsychiatric adverse events in people with stable psychiatric conditions including depression compared to placebo, nicotine patch, or bupropion. Standard titration is 0.5 mg daily x 3 days, 0.5 mg BD x 4 days, then 1 mg BD for 12 weeks, with quit date in week 2. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Nicotine patch 21 mg daily for 6 weeks plus inhaler for breakthrough cravings as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Combination NRT is reasonable but has lower cessation rates than varenicline; the patient has already failed NRT twice and is asking for the highest-efficacy option. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Switch sertraline to mirtazapine 30 mg nocte and add nicotine lozenges PRN as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Antidepressant switching is not indicated when depression is in remission, and mirtazapine has no established cessation benefit beyond placebo. (Source: RANZCP MDD)

options.text: Counselling alone with Quitline referral, deferring pharmacotherapy because of his depression history
options.rationale: Quitline counselling adds benefit but withholding pharmacotherapy because of stable in-remission depression is not supported by EAGLES; combining pharmacotherapy with behavioural support gives the highest cessation rates. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation)

explanation.summary: Varenicline is the most effective single smoking cessation agent and is safe in stable in-remission depression based on the EAGLES trial. Standard titration starts 1-2 weeks before quit date and continues for 12 weeks, with potential 12-week extension to maintain abstinence. Bupropion is second-line; combination NRT is reasonable when patient preference precludes varenicline. Behavioural support (Quitline) is added to pharmacotherapy, not used instead.
key_points: Varenicline first-line; EAGLES showed safety in stable depression
Titration: 0.5 mg daily x 3 days then 0.5 mg BD x 4 days then 1 mg BD
Quit date week 2; total course 12 weeks
Add Quitline behavioural support
explanation.pearls: EAGLES (NEJM 2016) found no excess neuropsychiatric adverse events with varenicline in people with stable psychiatric disorders, reversing a black-box warning that had previously discouraged its use.

stem:
A 22-year-old university student attends your GP clinic with binge eating followed by self-induced vomiting, occurring 4-5 times per week for the past 18 months. She also exercises excessively after binges and feels intense guilt and disgust afterwards. Her weight has been stable; BMI 22 kg/m^2. She has no purging-related complications such as hypokalaemia or oesophagitis on screening.

She has been engaged in CBT for eating disorders for 12 weeks with a clinical psychologist with partial improvement but ongoing binge-purge episodes 3-4 times per week. She is asking about medication options. She has no history of bipolar disorder, no suicidal ideation, no pregnancy plan in the next 12 months.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: BMI 22 kg/m^2, dental enamel erosion on the lingual surface of incisors, parotid swelling bilateral, Russell sign over knuckles minimal, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Potassium 3.9 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), bicarbonate 27 mmol/L (22-28), amylase 138 U/L (25-125), creatinine 64 micromol/L, magnesium 0.82 mmol/L, ECG sinus rhythm QTc 410 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Switch to interpersonal therapy and avoid pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: IPT is an alternative psychotherapy for bulimia but switching away from a partially effective CBT-ED course and avoiding pharmacotherapy contradicts the evidence base showing additional benefit from fluoxetine at 60 mg. (Source: RANZCP Eating Disorders)

options.text: Continue CBT and add fluoxetine 60 mg orally each morning
options.rationale: RANZCP and NICE eating disorder guidelines support fluoxetine 60 mg daily as adjunctive pharmacotherapy in bulimia nervosa when binge-purge symptoms persist despite a course of CBT-ED. The 60 mg dose is the evidence-supported anti-bulimic dose (higher than typical antidepressant dosing) and reduces binge and purge frequency by approximately 50% in trials. Continue psychotherapy alongside. (Source: RANZCP Eating Disorders)

options.text: Commence topiramate 100 mg twice daily for binge frequency
options.rationale: Topiramate has trial evidence in bulimia but is second-line because of cognitive adverse effects, weight loss in an otherwise weight-stable patient, and teratogenicity in a woman of reproductive age. (Source: RANZCP Eating Disorders)

options.text: Commence bupropion 150 mg daily as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Bupropion lowers seizure threshold and is contraindicated in bulimia nervosa because of an increased risk of seizures associated with purging-related electrolyte derangement. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Admit for inpatient refeeding and intensive psychotherapy
options.rationale: Inpatient admission is reserved for medical instability (severe electrolyte derangement, cardiac compromise) or treatment-resistant cases with safety concerns; this patient has stable physiology and is receiving outpatient psychotherapy. (Source: RANZCP Eating Disorders)

explanation.summary: Bulimia nervosa first-line treatment is CBT-ED. When CBT-ED is partially effective, fluoxetine 60 mg daily is the evidence-supported adjunctive pharmacotherapy and reduces binge-purge frequency by approximately 50%. Topiramate is second-line because of cognitive side effects and teratogenicity. Bupropion is contraindicated in bulimia. IPT is an alternative psychotherapy. Inpatient admission is reserved for medical instability.
key_points: Fluoxetine 60 mg daily is the anti-bulimic dose (NOT 20 mg)
CBT-ED is first-line; pharmacotherapy adjunctive
Bupropion contraindicated due to seizure risk
Topiramate second-line; teratogenic
explanation.pearls: Fluoxetine in bulimia uses the higher 60 mg dose to leverage the serotonergic anti-binge effect; the standard antidepressant 20 mg dose is inadequate for bulimia symptom suppression.

stem:
A 28-year-old man is referred by his GP after a recent confirmed diagnosis of adult ADHD by a consultant psychiatrist using DIVA-5 and confirmatory childhood school records. He reports lifelong difficulty sustaining attention, organisational struggles, and procrastination, with current impairment in his accounting job. He has no history of substance misuse, no cardiovascular disease, normal ECG, BP 124/78 mmHg, and no current depression. He drives a private vehicle for his commute (45 minutes each way).

He attends today to discuss starting pharmacotherapy. He has no insurance disqualifications and no pregnancy considerations. He has not previously trialled any ADHD medication.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 124/78 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Mental state normal cognition and mood, no psychotic features, no suicidality
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus rhythm 78, QTc 408 ms; FBC and UEC within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence bupropion 150 mg daily as off-label ADHD therapy as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Bupropion has limited evidence in adult ADHD and is not a first-line agent. RANZCP first-line is stimulants; atomoxetine and guanfacine are non-stimulant alternatives. (Source: RANZCP ADHD)

options.text: Commence atomoxetine 40 mg daily for 1 week then 80 mg daily, no notification required with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Atomoxetine is a reasonable non-stimulant first-line where stimulants are contraindicated or there is significant substance misuse risk, but it has lower effect size than stimulants in adult ADHD. Austroads requires notification for any ADHD treatment that affects driving capacity. (Source: RANZCP ADHD)

options.text: Commence lisdexamfetamine 30 mg orally each morning, baseline weight, and counsel on Austroads driving requirement to notify the licensing authority
options.rationale: RANZCP and AU ADHD guidelines recommend stimulants (methylphenidate or lisdexamfetamine) as first-line in adult ADHD without contraindication; lisdexamfetamine has smoother pharmacokinetics and lower abuse potential than immediate-release amphetamines. Austroads 'Assessing Fitness to Drive' requires self-notification to the driver licensing authority for ADHD on stimulant therapy; the condition itself does not disqualify but treatment must be reviewed. Baseline BP, weight, ECG already done. (Source: RANZCP ADHD)

options.text: Refer for CBT for ADHD and defer pharmacotherapy for 6 months as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Psychological therapy alone has smaller effect than pharmacotherapy in adult ADHD with functional impairment; the standard combined approach is medication plus psychological support and skill-building. (Source: RANZCP ADHD)

options.text: Commence methylphenidate immediate-release 10 mg three times daily without driving advice as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Although methylphenidate is a valid first-line stimulant, IR formulations require multiple daily dosing with greater rebound effect; sustained-release or extended-release preparations are preferred for adult work-and-drive lifestyles. Austroads notification is required regardless of agent. (Source: Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive)

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD first-line pharmacotherapy is stimulants - lisdexamfetamine or extended-release methylphenidate. Atomoxetine and guanfacine are non-stimulant alternatives for stimulant contraindication or substance misuse history. Baseline BP, pulse, ECG, weight, and Austroads notification are required. Combining pharmacotherapy with psychological support gives the best functional outcomes.
key_points: First-line: lisdexamfetamine or extended-release methylphenidate
Atomoxetine if stimulants contraindicated
Austroads requires driver notification on ADHD stimulant treatment
Baseline ECG, BP, pulse, weight before initiation
explanation.pearls: Austroads 'Assessing Fitness to Drive' treats ADHD as a notifiable condition when treatment may affect driving capacity; under-notification is a common practical compliance gap in AU practice.

stem:
A 38-year-old woman with a 15-year history of schizoaffective disorder, depressed type, is brought by her family because she has stopped eating, speaking, and moving for 4 days. She has been on quetiapine 600 mg nocte and sertraline 100 mg morning, both unchanged for 8 months. The family deny any obvious psychosocial trigger, medication change, or substance use.

In the assessment room she sits with one arm raised at shoulder height and maintains the posture when you adjust it. She does not respond to verbal questions, makes intermittent grimacing movements, and resists passive movement of her opposite arm by adopting an oppositional rigidity. Her examination is otherwise unremarkable; bloods including creatine kinase, electrolytes, and inflammatory markers are within normal limits.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96, BP 118/76 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 37.1 °C
data_table.Examination: Mutism, immobility, waxy flexibility, posturing of right arm, intermittent grimacing, gegenhalten of left arm, no rigidity at rest, no clonus, no autonomic instability
data_table.Investigations: Creatine kinase 92 U/L (<200), sodium 138 mmol/L (135-145), white cell count 7.4 x10^9/L (4-11), CRP 4 mg/L (<5), TSH 1.6 mIU/L; urine drug screen negative; CT brain within normal limits
data_table.Medications: Quetiapine 600 mg nocte, sertraline 100 mg morning

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Urgent electroconvulsive therapy referral as first-line without medication trial as the proposed approach
options.rationale: ECT is highly effective for catatonia and is escalated to when lorazepam is inadequate (typically 48-72 hours of 6-9 mg/day) or with malignant catatonia features. The first step is the diagnostic and therapeutic lorazepam challenge. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia)

options.text: Add olanzapine 10 mg IM and continue quetiapine with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Adding a second antipsychotic in suspected catatonia risks worsening catatonia and precipitating neuroleptic malignant syndrome. Antipsychotics are generally withheld until catatonia resolves; the first step is the lorazepam challenge. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia)

options.text: Commence dantrolene 1 mg/kg IV and bromocriptine 2.5 mg orally for suspected neuroleptic malignant syndrome
options.rationale: NMS requires hyperthermia, severe rigidity, autonomic instability, and markedly raised CK; this presentation has none of these. Treating presumptive NMS in confirmed catatonia delays correct therapy. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Lorazepam 2 mg IV/IM as a diagnostic challenge with reassessment of catatonia features in 15 minutes
options.rationale: She meets DSM-5 criteria for catatonia (mutism, immobility, waxy flexibility, posturing, grimacing, negativism). The lorazepam challenge - 2 mg IV or IM with reassessment at 5-15 minutes - is both diagnostic and therapeutic; a partial response within minutes confirms catatonia and directs treatment with scheduled lorazepam 2-3 mg three times daily titrated to response. CK is normal and there is no autonomic instability or hyperthermia, making NMS unlikely. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia)

options.text: Restart sertraline at higher dose (200 mg) and observe with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Increasing sertraline does not address catatonia and may worsen agitation or precipitate serotonin syndrome with no benefit. The diagnostic challenge with lorazepam is the next step. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia)

explanation.summary: Catatonia meeting DSM-5 criteria requires a lorazepam challenge (2 mg IV/IM, reassess at 5-15 min) which is both diagnostic and therapeutic; response confirms diagnosis and directs scheduled lorazepam 2-3 mg three times daily titrated up. Antipsychotics are generally withheld during active catatonia because of NMS risk and potential to worsen catatonia. ECT is escalated to for inadequate response or malignant features.
key_points: Lorazepam challenge 2 mg IV/IM, reassess 5-15 min
Confirmed catatonia: scheduled lorazepam 2-3 mg TDS titrated
Withhold antipsychotics during active catatonia
ECT for inadequate response or malignant catatonia
explanation.pearls: Catatonia in schizoaffective disorder is dramatic but the diagnostic test is bedside: 2 mg lorazepam within 15 minutes typically loosens posture and restores some speech if catatonia is the cause.

stem:
A 24-year-old woman is brought by ambulance to the emergency department 90 minutes after a deliberate self-poisoning. She reportedly ingested 30 tablets of amitriptyline 25 mg with a bottle of wine. She is drowsy with intermittent agitation. Her airway is patent and she is breathing spontaneously.

On the trolley she has an episode of generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 90 seconds, terminating with IV midazolam 5 mg. After the seizure her vital signs and ECG are obtained. The ECG shows sinus tachycardia with a QRS duration of 140 ms and a prominent terminal R wave in aVR.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 132, BP 86/52 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 96% on 6 L Hudson mask, temperature 37.2 °C
data_table.Examination: GCS 11 (E3V3M5) post-ictal, dilated pupils 6 mm sluggishly reactive, dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, decreased bowel sounds, urinary retention
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia, QRS 140 ms, terminal R in aVR 4 mm, QTc 470 ms; sodium 136 mmol/L, potassium 3.6 mmol/L, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L, lactate 4.6 mmol/L; venous gas pH 7.22, paracetamol level <10 mg/L, ethanol 0.18%
data_table.Medications: Pre-overdose: amitriptyline 50 mg nocte (prescribed for chronic pain)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 1-2 mmol/kg bolus, repeated to target QRS narrowing and arterial pH 7.50-7.55
options.rationale: QRS over 100 ms after TCA overdose indicates sodium channel blockade with elevated risk of seizure and ventricular arrhythmia; QRS over 120 ms or a terminal R wave in aVR over 3 mm are independent predictors. Hypertonic sodium bicarbonate (8.4% 1-2 mmol/kg bolus, repeated to narrow QRS or alkalise pH to 7.50-7.55) overcomes sodium channel blockade through both sodium loading and alkalinisation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology)

options.text: IV intralipid 20% bolus 1.5 mL/kg followed by infusion as first-line as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Intralipid is rescue therapy for cardiac arrest or refractory cardiotoxicity unresponsive to sodium bicarbonate, not first-line; sodium bicarbonate is the priority for QRS widening from TCA. (Source: Austin Toxicology Handbook)

options.text: IV amiodarone 300 mg loading dose for QRS widening with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Class III antiarrhythmics including amiodarone are contraindicated in TCA toxicity because they prolong QT and can worsen ventricular arrhythmia; class IA agents are also contraindicated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology)

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g orally now as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Activated charcoal beyond 1-2 hours post-ingestion is generally not effective and adds aspiration risk in a drowsy patient with anticholinergic ileus; this is 90 minutes post-ingestion with a depressed GCS, so airway protection would be required before charcoal. The priority is sodium bicarbonate for cardiotoxicity. (Source: Austin Toxicology Handbook)

options.text: Flumazenil 0.2 mg IV to reverse benzodiazepine sedation with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in TCA overdose because it lowers seizure threshold; this patient has already had one TCA-induced seizure and flumazenil would precipitate further seizures. (Source: Austin Toxicology Handbook)

explanation.summary: TCA overdose causes sodium-channel blockade with QRS widening, seizures, hypotension and ventricular arrhythmia. QRS >100 ms increases seizure risk, and >120 ms or terminal R wave in aVR >3 mm increase ventricular arrhythmia risk. Sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 1-2 mmol/kg bolus titrated to QRS narrowing and arterial pH 7.50-7.55 is first-line. Class I/III antiarrhythmics, flumazenil, and physostigmine are contraindicated. Intralipid is rescue for cardiac arrest only.
key_points: QRS >100 ms = seizure risk; >120 ms = arrhythmia risk
Terminal R in aVR >3 mm = arrhythmia risk
NaHCO3 8.4% 1-2 mmol/kg bolus, repeat to pH 7.50-7.55
Avoid Class I/III antiarrhythmics, flumazenil, physostigmine
explanation.pearls: In TCA cardiotoxicity, both the sodium load and the alkalinisation contribute to displacing the drug from sodium channels; targeting arterial pH 7.50-7.55 rather than 'normal' is the AU recommended endpoint.
